description: Refuse-or-cite Q&A GPC unit tests. Seeded from live club GPC Unit 16 Circuit Joining & Execution Google Form.

items.question: Why should a glider circuit be planned and flown using a recognised circuit pattern?
items.expected_answer: To support orderly traffic flow while preserving safe landing options.
choices: To ensure all pilots fly the same ground track regardless of wind or traffic.
To provide a predictable sequence while removing the need to adjust the circuit.
To support orderly traffic flow while preserving safe landing options.
To keep the glider close enough to the runway that normal lookout becomes less critical.

items.question: The formula given for setting approach speed at break-off from a soaring flight is:
items.expected_answer: 1.5 Vs + ½ wind speed.

items.question: Below 1 000 ft AGL you must fly at or above:
items.expected_answer: 1.5 Vs (“safe speed near the ground”).

items.question: By the completion of the turn onto final, the glider should ideally be:
items.expected_answer: Correctly configured, at the correct height and airspeed, ready to continue a stabilised approach.
choices: Correctly configured and at the correct airspeed, but slightly high so the approach can be adjusted later.
Correctly configured and at the correct height, with final approach speed selected once established on final.
Correctly configured, at the correct height and airspeed, but relying on significant airbrake use to achieve the desired approach.
Correctly configured, at the correct height and airspeed, ready to continue a stabilised approach.

items.question: Which GPC unit is listed as the only prerequisite for Unit 16?
items.expected_answer: Unit 15 — Break-off & Circuit Planning.

items.question: Two complementary units the Guide recommends studying with Unit 16 are:
items.expected_answer: Unit 17 & Unit 21.

items.question: The pre-landing checks should be completed:
items.expected_answer: By mid-downwind leg.
choices: As the glider passes abeam the intended landing area.

items.question: If the runway angle seen out the side window looks too steep while down-wind, the recommended correction is to:
items.expected_answer: Turn 20–30° away from the strip briefly, then parallel again.; Open airbrakes for a few seconds.
choices: Turn toward the strip for a few seconds.
Turn 20–30° away from the strip briefly, then parallel again.
Lower the nose and speed up.
Open airbrakes for a few seconds.
correct_choices: Turn 20–30° away from the strip briefly, then parallel again.
Open airbrakes for a few seconds.
items.note: Pilot Guide: turn 20–30° away when angle too steep. Trainer also lists airbrake use among steep/shallow circuit corrections.

items.question: Conversely, if the angle appears too shallow you should:
items.expected_answer: Turn toward the strip for a few seconds, then parallel again.
choices: Turn toward the strip for a few seconds, then parallel again.

items.question: In strong winds the base-leg turn should be made:
items.expected_answer: Earlier than usual to avoid drifting too far away.
choices: Earlier than usual to avoid drifting too far away.
At exactly the same point as calm days.

items.question: Where the circuit direction is not prescribed, which circuit side is generally preferred in crosswind conditions?
items.expected_answer: The side where the wind blows the glider away from the strip on downwind.
choices: The side where the wind blows the glider toward the strip on downwind.
The side that provides the shortest possible base leg.
The side where the wind blows the glider away from the strip on downwind.
The side that requires the greatest drift correction on the base leg.

items.question: If unexpected heavy sink means you cannot reach the planned final path, you must:
items.expected_answer: Turn in early, select a safe landing area down-field and fly a modified circuit.
choices: Increase speed and dive for it.
Turn in early, select a safe landing area down-field and fly a modified circuit.

items.question: Overshooting the turn onto final is most likely to result in:
items.expected_answer: A steep correcting turn and poor alignment with the landing area centreline.
choices: A longer final approach with more time to stabilise.
A steep correcting turn and poor alignment with the landing area centreline.

items.question: During the circuit your primary attitude reference for holding approach speed is the:
items.expected_answer: Nose attitude relative to the horizon.
choices: Nose attitude relative to the horizon.

items.question: While flying the circuit, making a radio call must never take priority over:
items.expected_answer: Maintaining control of the glider.
choices: Keeping other aircraft informed of your intentions.

items.question: A targeted scan in the circuit should include a particular lookout for:
items.expected_answer: Traffic approaching from an opposite circuit or on long final.
choices: Traffic approaching from an opposite circuit or on long final.
Traffic taxiing near the launch point.

items.question: If the landing area is seen to become obstructed while on base leg, you should first:
items.expected_answer: Modify the circuit or choose another safe area on the aerodrome.
choices: Modify the circuit or choose another safe area on the aerodrome.

items.question: If a crosswind on the final approach produces a tailwind component on the base leg (i.e. the wind is from behind during the base leg), the turn onto final should normally be commenced:
items.expected_answer: Earlier than normal.

items.question: Competence for this unit requires arriving on final no lower than:
items.expected_answer: 300 ft AGL.

items.question: A common student error is flying the same joining point every flight. The root cause is:
items.expected_answer: Using a rote pattern instead of assessing wind, traffic and obstacles each time.
choices: Using a rote pattern instead of assessing wind, traffic and obstacles each time.
items.note: Rote joining pattern is discussed in Unit 15 common problems; Unit 16 reinforces reassessment.
